1.title: MachinaCheck: Building a Multi-Agent CNC Manufacturability System on AMD MI300X
1.author: Hugging Face

2.title: Mothers who build
2.author: Replit
2.description:
A stream of consciousness By Haya Odeh, Co-founder & Head of Design, Replit May 2026

   

From the moment I got married, the question started.

When are you having children?

Then, once I had them: How are you still doing this? Why do you even need a job? Your husband is providing.

I heard versions of that my entire career. The assumption underneath it, that a woman's ambition has a natural stopping point, that motherhood is where the building ends, is something I've been quietly arguing against for over a decade. Let me try to form my thoughts around this in answers to questions I’ve heard from others or pondered myself. Questions that mothers out there can relate to.

7.title: Agentic UX: 7 principles for designing systems with agents
7.author: ‍ Alexandra Vasquez
7.description:
Agents don’t need their own screen, they need better systems to operate in

The surface designers work on is changing. We are still designing interfaces for people, but we are also shaping the systems underneath them. We don’t need to become engineers or experts in every new technology, but we do need to adapt.

Agentic AI goes beyond responding to prompts. It plans, uses tools, and takes action toward a goal. Agentic UX (a.k.a. AX) is the design response to that shift: structuring systems so agents can operate in the background and meaningfully accelerate human work. This article is a practical synthesis of what has held up for my teams and I while building real systems, testing concept ideas, influencing product direction, and helping others adjust their mindset as the ground keeps moving.

Agentic UX principles for design

1. Let’s think systematically

AI is only as good as the data it gets. I learned this the hard way while working on a prioritization agent meant to speed up resolution times. Instead, it slowed the team and delayed a release.

The issue wasn’t the model. The platform itself was confusing. Designing for agents means treating information architecture and workflows as foundational. Before building an agent, audit your system’s foundations: Are labels consistent? Do hierarchies make sense? Can a new team member navigate workflows without constant help? If humans struggle, agents will fail faster and at scale. Fix the system first.

When the team learned to think more systematically, regardless of role, we were able to move the features forward and lay better groundwork for future AI implementations.

Many issues that surface as “AI problems” are actually long standing system design problems that only become visible once an agent is introduced.

2. Agents should be invisible, not absent

Agents should blend into existing workflows. I’ve watched teams add AI buttons to every screen, launch chatbots in sidebars, and plaster “Try AI!” banners across dashboards. Users shouldn’t have to go somewhere else to get value. Ideally, the product simply feels smarter by default.

Invisible does not mean hidden. It means the agent doesn’t require its own destination. It works within the product, not next to it. Good invisible automation: Gmail’s Smart Compose. You’re typing an email, suggestions appear inline, you press tab to accept or keep typing to ignore. No separate AI assistant window. No “Use AI?” prompt. Just the product, slightly smarter.

If an AI feature feels bolted on, requires a detour, or exists only because “we need AI,” it probably doesn’t belong.

3. Shift from reactive to proactive

Traditional generative AI reacts to input. Agentic systems understand goals, break work into steps, and move things forward.

While building three agents at once, we hit a problem that had nothing to do with the models. We were unsure how and when to prompt users to interact with our agents at all. Too many interruptions felt aggressive. Too few made the system feel passive.

Proactive doesn’t mean louder. It means recognizing patterns, applying context, and offering the next useful step before the user has to ask, without taking control away. Proactiveness in practice: A project management tool notices three tasks are blocked waiting on the same dependency. Instead of waiting for you to dig through the board, it surfaces a single notification: “Design review is blocking 3 tasks. Schedule it now?” One click, multiple problems solved.

Reactive AI answers questions. Proactive agents move work forward.

4. Context makes or breaks the experience

Agents only work well when context flows both ways.

Good Agentic UX treats context as a shared surface. Information flows into the agent so it can act meaningfully, making actions intelligent instead of random. That same information must flow back to the user in the place where work is already happening. Context is what makes agent behavior feel trustworthy.

When we first shipped agents, we tried everything to get users to notice them. Slideouts. Banners. Links to separate spaces. By the time a user went to the agent, resolved their issue, and returned to the original screen, the experience felt disjointed.

Agents shouldn’t require a separate mental space or switching modes. Most agent failures are not intelligence failures, they are context failures.

Here’s what context failures look like in practice:

Scenario 1: Setup context
Product: Project management tool
Action: User selects “Website redesign” during setup
❌ Avoid: The agent asks for the project type again before making recommendations.
Result: User thinks “I just told you this” and loses trust.

Scenario 2: Inline Changes
Product: Task or issue tracker
Action: The agent updates priority on multiple items.
❌ Avoid: A message says “Changes were made by AI” and links to a separate assistant panel to explain them.
Result: User has to leave their workflow to understand what happened in their workflow.

Context successes look like this:

Scenario 1: Setup context
Product: Project management tool
Action: User selects “Website redesign” during setup
✅ Good: The agent later prioritizes tasks related to content, navigation, and QA without asking what kind of project this is.
Result: The agent remembers. Work moves faster.

Scenario 2: Inline Changes
Product: Task or issue tracker
Action: The agent updates priority on multiple items.
✅ Good: The new priorities appear directly in the list with a small “Updated by AI” label and an undo option.
Result: User sees what changed, why it changed, and can reverse it without breaking focus.

5. Use known patterns

Do not reinvent the wheel.

Teams rush to design new components the moment AI touches a feature. I’ve done this too. Here’s what that looked like: We built a custom AI panel with its own navigation, input styles, and button treatments. It looked “futuristic” in the prototype. In user testing, people kept asking where things were and how to get back to their actual work. We had created a separate product inside our product.

The fix was simple: we deleted the custom panel and put agent actions in the same menus, modals, and toolbars people already used. Familiarity lowers cognitive load and makes adoption easier.

Companies have spent years studying interaction patterns. Your team has likely done the same. That knowledge doesn’t suddenly become irrelevant just because AI enters the picture.

Agent interactions should look and feel like the rest of the product. Here are some good familiarity patterns examples:

  • Slack’s AI appears in the same /command structure users already know.
  • Notion’s AI uses the same slash menu for both manual and AI actions.

And some anti-patterns to avoid:

  • Custom AI chat bubbles or special “AI buttons” that look nothing like your product’s UI.
  • Separate AI workspaces that pull users out of their normal flow.

6. Collect the right data at the right time

Agents depend on inputs. Missing or late information makes even good systems feel unreliable. I’ve built agents for data-heavy enterprise products and scrappy MVPs. Both extremes hurt long-term behavior. Too much data collection and nobody fills it out. Too little and the agent guesses randomly

Most agent failures aren’t intelligence failures. They are input failures. Data was never captured, captured too late, or forgotten.

A helpful way to think about this is through Microsoft’s concept of Agent Time:

Past: what the system has already captured. Preferences, goals, prior decisions.
Present: what is happening right now. Current state, constraints, signals.
Future: how the agent adapts based on accumulated inputs and outcomes.

Designers influence all three. When we decide what to capture, when to capture it, and where to surface it again, we shape how intelligent the system can actually become.

7. Keep humans in control and ensure agents stay transparent

Agents hallucinate, misunderstand context, and make mistakes. That is expected. Design should account for it. Agents are meant to help humans, not replace them. They can make work faster, but users remain the decision makers.

Control means visibility, reversibility, and choice. Users should see what happened, undo it, and decide when automation applies. Early solutions can leverage logs, indicators, undo actions, and embedded feedback loops often build trust faster than hidden magic.

Transparency is not about showing everything. It’s about showing enough, in the right place, at the right time. A simple check helps: would a user understand what just happened without extra effort? If not, trust is already at risk.

Full transparency example: Grammarly’s inline suggestions. You see the change, see why it’s suggested, accept or reject with one click, and it learns from your choices. No hidden magic. No breaking your flow. Just visible, reversible assistance.

In practice:

Scenario: Automated Changes.
Product: Configuration or settings screen.
Action: The agent updates a rule automatically.
✅ Good: The change appears inline with a small indicator, a short explanation, and an undo option.
❌ Avoid: The rule changes silently and the user only notices later when something breaks.

What this all mean in practice

The real work often starts before wireframes or tickets. Before my team builds an agent, we now map the information flow first:

  1. What data exists but isn’t captured? (Add inputs)
  2. What data exists but isn’t surfaced? (Fix context)
  3. What decisions require human judgment? (Don’t automate)
  4. What’s repetitive and rule-based? (Automate here)

This mapping happens in a Miro/Figjam/white boards with designers, PMs, and engineers. The AI model is the last thing we discuss, not the first. These are product decisions, and designers have outsized influence here.

Agentic UX is less about adding intelligence and more about designing clarity, structure, and accountability into the system itself.

Question AI when it feels forced. Focus on the end-to-end experience. Influence teams to take all these into account when building AI agents.🤨

Hey, I’m Alex

Everything in this article comes from hands on experience. From figuring out the process, and building something my whole team could leverage. From pushing back when AI showed up mostly to look good in a deck. From discovering, sometimes the hard way, where agents actually help and what makes them work best.

As a product person these ideas are the same ones I end up talking, and sometimes ranting, about with friends and colleagues, both techy friends and very much not. I care about making tech feel less mystical and more grounded. Less hype, more usefulness.

If this helps you push back a little more confidently, design better systems, and free up some time to step away from the noise for a bit, then it’s done what I hoped it would.

Resources

The thinking in this article builds on work from teams actively designing and deploying agentic systems. If you want to go deeper:

  • Microsoft — Agentic Design Patterns
    https://microsoft.github.io/ai-agents-for-beginners/03-agentic-design-patterns/
  • IBM — What is Agentic AI
    https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/agentic-ai
  • Carbon Design System — Designing for AI
    https://carbondesignsystem.com/guidelines/carbon-for-ai/
  • Arion Research — Perspectives on Agentic AI
    https://www.arionresearch.com/blog/f0cl762e75x6icp6psj4dgdbewp4ik
  • AWS — Your AI is Only as Good as Your Data
    https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/enterprise-strategy/your-ai-is-only-as-good-as-your-data/

Agentic UX: 7 principles for designing systems with agents was originally published in Bootcamp on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

8.title: The Cost of Rework No One Talks About
8.author: @mohitphogat
8.description:
Everyone counts the hours. Nobody counts what the hours cost beyond the clock.

Here’s a number that stopped me cold when I first saw it: 30 to 50 percent of all effort on software projects is spent on rework.

Not building. Not shipping. Redoing work that was already done.

That stat comes from software measurement research that’s been replicated across multiple studies — and yet somehow, it doesn’t come up in sprint planning, or project retrospectives, or any of the conversations where teams talk about why things are taking longer than expected.

Because rework has a great cover story. It gets filed under “iteration.” It gets called “part of the process.” It hides inside phrases like “we need to revisit this” and “requirements changed.” It’s practically invisible on a project tracker, because it looks exactly like regular work. New tickets, same effort, different outcome.

But the hours on the ticket aren’t the real cost. The real cost is everything the hours can’t capture.

What the tracker doesn’t show

When a team reworks a feature, the time is obvious. Four days to build it, three days to rebuild it — that’s seven days total. The budget takes the hit. The deadline slips. That part gets noticed.

What doesn’t get noticed is that reworked code is slower to write than original code. Research from software measurement firm ScopeMaster found that productivity on rework runs about 25% lower than on initial builds — because you’re not writing from scratch, you’re navigating decisions already made, trying to preserve what still works while changing what doesn’t. You’re reading your own old code, or someone else’s, trying to figure out where to cut without breaking something else. That takes longer. It always takes longer.

And it compounds. A medium-sized engineering team can lose upward of $4.7 million a year to unnecessary rework, according to DORA research — and that figure only accounts for the wasted hours. It doesn’t account for delayed features, missed market windows, or the work that didn’t happen because the team was busy going backwards.

The cost that doesn’t fit on a spreadsheet

The harder cost to measure — and the one almost no one talks about — is what rework does to the people doing it.

Nearly three-quarters of software developers experience burnout at some point in their careers, according to data from Runn’s engineering productivity research. That’s significantly higher than the average burnout rate across all industries. And while there are many contributing factors, constant rework is consistently listed among the top triggers.

It’s not hard to understand why. There’s a specific kind of exhaustion that comes from redoing work you’ve already done. It’s different from the tiredness that comes with a hard project. Hard projects feel like progress. Rework feels like running on a treadmill — you’re working just as hard, but you’re not going anywhere.

Over time, that feeling kills motivation. Developers who deal with frequent rewrites show higher rates of disengagement, and disengagement leads to lower code quality — which leads to more rework, as flagged in Hatica’s 2024 engineering benchmark research. It’s a cycle that feeds itself, and most teams never connect the two ends of it.

There’s also a specific toll on senior developers. Stack Overflow’s 2024 developer survey of 65,000+ respondents flagged something worth paying attention to: for the first time, senior developers reported lower job satisfaction than junior ones. A big reason cited was the volume of maintenance, remediation, and repeated work. The developers with the most context — the ones who understand the system the deepest — are spending their time redoing instead of building. That’s not just a morale problem. That’s a waste of the most expensive people on the team.

Why it keeps happening

Rework isn’t usually random. It tends to come from a few predictable sources.

Vague requirements signed off too early. When teams start building on requirements that haven’t been properly clarified, they’re making assumptions at every decision point. Those assumptions get built into the code. When someone eventually catches the gap — a stakeholder review, a usability test, a demo — the assumption has to be undone. The later in the process that happens, the more expensive the undo.

ScopeMaster’s research consistently finds that poor communication — not genuinely changing business needs — is the root cause of most rework. Requirements that felt clear in a meeting weren’t. Nobody pushed back. The build started anyway.

Feedback arriving at the wrong stage. Design decisions are cheap to change on paper. They’re expensive to change in code. And they’re very expensive to change after the code is in production. When stakeholders give feedback late — which they often do, because they need to see something built before they know what they want — the rework happens at the most costly possible point.

Technical debt borrowed in a hurry. Shortcuts taken to hit a deadline don’t disappear. They stay in the codebase, and eventually someone has to come back for them. Developers spend an average of 13.5 hours per week on technical debt alone — cited across multiple 2024 engineering productivity reports. That’s a third of the working week, every week, going to past decisions.

The team cost nobody budgets for

One more cost that doesn’t appear on any tracker: the people who leave because of it.

When teams spend more time rebuilding than building, the developers who care most — the ones who want to ship things they’re proud of — start to disengage first. They’re the ones most frustrated by the waste. According to Gartner’s 2024 Workforce Productivity Report, each developer who leaves sets a team back four to eight weeks in delivery time. That’s the timeline cost alone, before counting recruiting, onboarding, knowledge transfer, and the months before a new hire is fully productive.

And teams that experience high turnover accumulate 37% more technical debt and spend 22% more time debugging than stable teams, per LinearB’s 2024 Engineering Productivity Report. So the rework cycle gets worse after a departure, not better — because the institutional knowledge that could have prevented the next round of rework walked out the door with the person who left.

None of this is inevitable. Teams that catch requirements problems early, get feedback at the design stage instead of the production stage, and treat technical debt as a real line item in their planning show dramatically lower rework rates. The tooling to measure it — git-level code churn analysis, sprint tracking, structured retrospectives — already exists. The commitment to actually use it is the harder part.

What this actually costs you

Put it together and the picture is clearer than most project post-mortems make it:

  • Reworked code takes 25% longer to write than original code
  • 30–50% of total project effort on most teams is rework
  • Developer burnout — heavily linked to rework — affects nearly 75% of developers at some point
  • Each developer who leaves costs the team 4–8 weeks of delivery time
  • Teams with high turnover accumulate 37% more technical debt, feeding more rework

The hours are just the start. The real cost is compounding — and most of it happens silently, between the tickets, below the line items, in the places no one is measuring.

The first step to reducing it is deciding to actually track it. How much of last sprint was new work? How much was revisiting something already built? Most teams don’t know the answer. That’s not a data problem. That’s a choice about what to pay attention to.

If you found this useful, follow me here. I write about development, design, and the things that actually affect how teams build — not just the technical side, but the human cost that shows up in sprint velocity before anyone names it.
